raw_payload.author: Aki10
raw_payload.content:
Namby pamby, ungrateful, wrong place, wrong time and wrong decision-making crybaby family irritated me throughout the movie. Would've been better with ScarJo and her team in my opinion.

Dinos were good, but action scenes, background roars/growls could've been more - let a dino movie BE a dino movie.

raw_payload.author: druss44121-1
raw_payload.content:
I didn't go in with very high expectations, but this still should have been better considering David Koepp was returning as a writer and Gareth Edwards is a good choice for director. I even liked his 2014 Godzilla movie more than most people, so I am far from a hater.

I'll break this down into three pros and cons to keep this short.

Pros:

1) There are dinosaurs.

2) Some great location photography, especially in Thailand.

3) There are a couple of great moments of grandeur, although the best one for me (revealing a herd in a valley) relies on a lot of nostalgia farming. Still, it's effective. There's also a sequence with a raft that feels like Spielberg could have directed it, in a good way.

Cons:

1) The script is bad. Lots of exposition, despite the fact that the plot is very simplistic. Some of the characters are annoying. Everything is very predictable, to the point that there's really not a single surprise. There's little tension or sense of danger or even mystery.

2) The cast. Most of them seem to be here to collect a paycheck. Scarlett Johansson seems a little miscast for the character she's playing, though I'd still primarily blame the writing rather than her performance. Jonathan Bailey is a non-entity. Mahershala Ali is wasted. None of these characters are memorable. The best character is probably a tiny dinosaur they meet along the way that gets a few easy "aww" moments. It's cheap, but it works.

3) The special effects. As far as I could tell, the dinosaurs are 100% CGI (or close to it). Dominion was far from good but the mix of practical and CGI effects was nice to see, and a step in the right direction. I was hoping for more of that here, especially since there are several opportunities for practical effects once they get on the island. Everything looks artificial. It's also one of those movies where despite the great location photography, some of the environments still look really fake.

Anyway... the last three movies in this franchise have all been pretty bad. I don't expect something on par with the first Jurassic Park, but I think we could do a little better than this!

5/10.

raw_payload.author: southdavid
raw_payload.content:
It's rare I see films on their opening day, opening weekend is relatively common but - as I'm otherwise engaged this weekend, I decided to see "Jurassic World Rebirth" on a Wednesday evening. I wish I could say it was because of enthusiasm for the series, but that has largely been beaten out of me at this point. I wish I could say that Rebirth was a film that reignited my love of the franchise but, whilst it's fine, it's largely another retread.

Dinosaurs have proven ill adjusted to the climate of most modern countries, with those that have survived clustering around the equator. Martin Krebs (Rupert Friend) representative of ParkerGenix believes that genetic material from three live dinosaurs will help with the research into heart disease that his company are planning. He hires Zora Bennett (Scarlett Johannson) and Henry Loomis (Jonathan Bailey) as lead and scientific expert of his expedition, and they head to the island of Ile Saint-Hubert. But the island was InGen's genetics experiment hub, abandoned following an incident years earlier.

To stress, I didn't think that "Rebirth" was a bad film. I thought the central performances were good and I liked the chemistry between Johansson and Bailey and the endless charisma of Mahershala Ali. I thought the film looked really good Gareth Edwards is a great visual film maker and has plenty of experience with large scale creatures.

The problem is that it's essentially 'another' Jurassic movie and feels like its plot is cribbed almost entirely from existing elements of the series. The stranded family from "Jurassic World 3", the experts from "Lost World", the really dangerous 'final boss' creature from "World" - with a nod towards the jails underneath Jabba's palace.

Without any sort of new angle the word I kept coming back to when thinking about the film was 'inessential' and, once again whilst this was fine, I do feel like the whole franchise could benefit from a lengthy break.

raw_payload.author: trav271
raw_payload.content: I wish I could take a previous review for a Jurassic park clone and insert it here to get across the effort the studio is putting into these now. It's just a cheap cash in for some money. Very boring and predictable. The CGI actually looked worse than the 1993 movie. How is that even possible you ask? By just having gratuitous CGI constantly. It just looks bad and I'm actually depressed after watching this. Something about it all just felt cynical and like it somehow ruined my love of the original a little bit. With that said, this is the last ride for me on this franchise. It hasn't been good for decades as I look back and think about these films.

raw_payload.author: TheCinemagoer
raw_payload.content:
As someone who's watched every entry in the Jurassic Park and Jurassic World franchises, I have to say, this latest installment is the most disappointing of them all. But should you skip it entirely? Absolutely not. It still works as a decent popcorn flick if you're just in the mood for some dino-action without expecting much else.

When Scarlett Johansson was announced as part of the cast, I had high hopes for a standout performance, especially given her strong track record and the charisma Chris Pratt brought to the previous Jurassic World films. Unfortunately, the film doesn't give her much to work with, and her potential feels underutilized.

What really lets this movie down is its tone. The signature humor that helped balance the tension in earlier films is noticeably absent. Even worse, the horror and suspense hallmarks of any good dinosaur movie are dialed way down, leaving the action feeling flat.

The story itself is a mess. It stumbles at the start, never quite finds its rhythm, and ends without impact. The plot feels disjointed, and the characters are poorly developed, you never feel emotionally invested in what's happening.

And then there's the CGI. For a franchise known for groundbreaking visual effects, this entry is shockingly subpar. The effects are shabby and unconvincing, which really takes you out of the experience.

Bottom line: it's a missed opportunity in almost every way. Still, if you're a fan of the series or just want to see dinosaurs on screen, it's worth a one-time watch, just lower your expectations.

raw_payload.author: bahae19
raw_payload.content:
Honestly, the first half felt all over the place - too much talking, too many introductions, and pacing that dragged. But once they hit the island, it got better: dinosaur action scenes were intense and visual effects impressive, especially that river chase and cliff sequence.

Scarlett Johansson and Jonathan Bailey were solid, with Bailey bringing unexpected charm despite some forced emotional lines. The movie leans heavily on spectacle and nostalgia, yet the human characters felt shallow and underwritten - I never truly cared about them.

It's entertaining as a summer blockbuster if you just want dinosaur chaos, but it lacks depth. Still, worth the watch for the action and visuals.

raw_payload.author: kakuraka
raw_payload.content:
I'm genuinely furious. This film is a full-on disaster for common sense. Everything is absurdly stupid - from the script to the characters' behavior.

There's hardly any plot to speak of. Instead of a coherent story, we get a pathetic excuse for intrigue - something about "collecting blood samples." The rest is just empty space, disconnected scenes, and aimless noise. It feels like the writers just gave up, hoping that big names and dinosaurs would carry the film.

But even the dinosaurs are missing. What we get instead are grotesque mutant creatures that inspire neither fear nor awe - only disgust. Where's the grandeur of the ancient world? Where's the spirit of adventure? Everything is drowned in a CGI mess.

And let's not forget the behavior of the main characters. It's not just stupid - it's insulting. It feels like they're competing to see who can make the dumbest decision every minute. Watching it is physically painful.

And then there's the architecture and technology. Are you kidding me? You're on an island full of predators, and everything makes noise - doors bang, systems whir, everything clatters. It's as if the designers intentionally made it easier for dinosaurs to find and eat everyone. Total nonsense.

In conclusion: this film is hollow, annoying, and completely disrespectful to the audience. No plot, no sensible characters, no proper dinosaurs. Just noise, stupidity, and monstrosities. A "rebirth"... into something much, much worse.

The dinos easily gave up, seemingly for no reason. Possibly "I got bored of chasing you," or "it isn't that convenient to keep chasing you." Other than the beginning of the movie, I never felt like the characters were in real danger, it wasn't scary like the original trilogy.

raw_payload.author: imdbfan-175926
raw_payload.content:
1. Let's start with the trailers. They gave away so much of the movie, so now you're just waiting to see it take place. It took all the suspense out a film that desperately needed some.

2. Characters. As many have said, they're forgettable. The only character I remember the name of was Nina - and that's because despite seeing her dragged underwater by a dinosaur 50 times her size, her colleague kept calling for her as if she was going to stroll back onto the beach. There was a French guy who we knew would die. The classic trigger-happy mr. Ego mercenary was a snack in waiting.

The villain was weak with no depth - and he wasn't even that bad. But I'm 99% sure he didn't need to make the trip to the island. He kinda didn't want to save the girl for fear she'd ruin the plan, but he could have just come up with a better lie and saved her... Their feud wasn't resolved.

The family was a huge mistake. Fine to show reckless travellers being fish food, but now we must put up with Dora the Explorer carrying a weighty little dinosaur in her backpack for zero reason. If I'm on an island with my daughters post capsizing to rabid dinosaurs and seeing multiple people die, my daughter isn't leaving my side. I'm not letting her meander off to befriend a wild mutant animal for speech therapy. Boyfriend has no arc.

Dino doctor was crying seeing a dinosaur (for a stupidly long scene where SJ struggles with a weapon and you sense they'll be a stampede but there isn't) despite a similar sauropod chilling in Brooklyn.

I've just remembered SJ's character's name. Z... cool vague backstory that has no connection with the movie whatsoever.

3. The plot. 6 JPs put Dinosaurs around the world. One rebirth kills them all and we're back to Costa Rica. Great. What a waste. They need dino blood for heart disease...only the biggest animals on land, air and sea can provide... despite t-rex having a bigger heart than anything that flies, we still need the bird. Yawn. Get there, get back, everyone is rich. 10 with 6 zeros, not including the zero in the ten... money.

A family sailed near the island, but a multi-billion dollar company with extensive connections needs a random crew of misfits and a battleship... which they drive into shallow water and make everyone jump out of unnecessarily early.

Sans gun-guy and Nina, the crew have no issue wandering deep into the island, climbing immense cliffs, and abseiling erratically to the floor below. (How in the hell did they walk down those stairs so quickly... and why would they?)

Guy 'takes a piss' miles from anyone, despite saying he said he'd tend the fire they somehow constructed, so then leaves, but the dad instantly falls into an unwakeable sleep continuing a pattern of not protecting his daughters, so his youngest can feed a tiny herbivore junk food and befriend it. They're not on high ground, raptors are super close, including a flying one that eats other raptors, and ... we're just cool with that. Weak nod to Alan Grant tree scene with kids. Otherwise no explanation as to how everyone survived the night.

Anyway, Dad then let's oldest go and get a boat by herself (which she'd never be able to move alone) despite not being in a hurry. They all see a giant trex but don't retreat, just frantically wave at oldest daughter who finds a working torch and a raft, which she then drags over a pier, despite not needing to, and then opens it, doesn't move, and the giant trex vanishes... Fixated on the stupid raft, they all paddle downstream away from a dinosaur that can swim 3 times as fast as them, but doesn't kill them, but nearly does, but only the dad, and not the other two, and then the boat is fine, but the youngest emerges, but gets away from the dinosaur, and then they laugh. And the boat is fine.

And then they all shout and scream to find one another and don't run away or hide or stay together or do anything remotely sensible when hearing loud ominous noises. Then you see the only slightly intelligent one taking his time to stuff his pockets with flares before actually just lighting one of them as his only exit plan flies away - anyway, then magically flexible and determined flying raptor-dinos relentlessly chase not their natural food source into tunnels and annoying older daughter screams but doesn't move, and the little sister who hardly pays attention to anyone now knows how to open a gate she's never seen work, and then man who was at the dock is now hurtling towards the world's largest carnivore like a crazed and uncontrollable moron, and then gets eaten like the pig solider from star wars, but despite not knowing how the DNA will solve heart disease Scarlet insists on grabbing the suitcase his hand is still attached to and runs to the dock which was definitely miles away from their starting location and we're still none the wiser as to why the child has the pet. But then giant mutant dino hesitates to eat tiny child for no reason and suddenly they've realised it likes flares, so man lights one and takes a while to contemplate his heroism before he swims with one hand as he holds a flare and leads dinosaur 100 times his size into a swamp. Then family and remaining characters take perfectly working boat with gas and a working generator out into the darkness, only to see another flare shot a half mile from the previous spot, despite the other dinosaur still being super close and a fan of flares, they turn their boat light on and save the laughing man who in no way is taking any of this seriously, and then they ride out at sunrise, despite the entire previous scene being shot just after sunset.

4. Plot holes. Well, plenty. Man loudly crunches Altoids for 30 seconds as if he's eaten the hole tin. Man hurts leg, which has no impact on the story, but despite saying he can't move it he does jump from a boat and swim to shore then hike for a day and a half across a mountainous jungle. Loving father tells his oldest daughter to grab youngest daughter in the final scenes, despite he and daughter's boyfriend being just as close. Man has handcuffs and uses them, despite nobody questioning him having control of the suitcase. Two men descend 800+ feet down ancient staircase around the same time as a man freefalls half the distance. Speeding mosasaur can't catch self-proclaimed 'lazy' man with bag. All torches work. Extraction team sensibly choose nightfall to air-search for survivors. The snickers wrapper. Terrible friend & key buddy. Magic fire. Terrible father. Undamaged raft. Perfectly kept grocery store. Holding a door against a giant dinosaur. Loud car annoys villain driver but has no impact on the rest of the story. Car works.

5. It's true, this is the worst written movie of the 7, and in the last movie they had flying fire locusts and Tim Apple. Watch it for the 6 or so dinosaurs. But be prepared to choke on your snickers bar as you struggle to comprehend this abomination of a movie.

raw_payload.author: thepiczr
raw_payload.content:
Five years after the mess that was Jurassic World: Dominion (2022), we're back in the land of toothy nostalgia. This time, an expedition heads into remote equatorial regions to extract DNA from three colossal prehistoric creatures in the name of a "groundbreaking medical breakthrough."

Chris Pratt and Bryce Dallas Howard are mercifully gone (finally), and in their place we get Scarlett Johansson, stepping in as the new face of the franchise for round seven of the Jurassic Park/World saga. Gareth Edwards takes the director's chair, and while he's always known how to stage a good spectacle, even his talents can't fully save this dino-soaked popcorn flick.

Let's talk visuals. There's certainly no shortage of giant set pieces and dinosaur mayhem-but when your dinosaurs look like glossy, overcooked cartoon rejects, all the action in the world can't make it feel real. Hollywood still hasn't learned that audiences are burnt out on soulless green-screen overloads, and the actors, forced to react to nothing, stumble through half-baked expressions of terror. You can practically see them wondering where the tennis ball on a stick went.

Character-wise, it's a wash. Most of them are either grating or entirely forgettable, spouting cringey, rapid-fire dialogue that exists solely to shuttle us from one set piece to the next. And while Rebirth does manage to attempt something new and different, it still feels like we're just circling the same prehistoric drain. It's entertaining, sure-but it just isn't that exciting.

That said, it's still a step up from Fallen Kingdom and Dominion, which isn't saying much, but at least it's something. At this point, though, it's time Universal grew a pair and gave us the R-rated, blood-splattered dinosaur horror epic we deserve-because the stakes never feel high when your carnivores are sanitized for mass consumption.

Here's hoping the next one finally evolves. But I'm not holding my breath. It's best to turn your brain off and enjoy this one for what it is.

6/10.

raw_payload.author: MattStGelais
raw_payload.content:
Stupidity from beginning to end. I can't remember the last time a movie actually made me angry. I was actively rooting for the characters to die. How did a 60+ft dinosaur hide in 5ft of grass? An, entire security system completely disabled by a Snickers wrapper?

I honestly hope that this is the end of the franchise.

I saw this for free with Regal Unlimited, and I still want my money back. Everyone involved in the production should be ashamed of themselves.

The end.

raw_payload.author: resrwa
raw_payload.content:
Jurassic Park remains my favorite movie of all time. I did not hate TLW and JW was a solid entry. But what started with awe and wonder at dinosaurs we recognize has become more about random monsters with JP-universe names to tie it in. This was not a bad film. But it was mediocre. The first half was slow with forced backstories to give characters some skin in the game. The magic of Koepp and Crichton was lost after the first movie and with Crichton's passing we'll never get another chance.

raw_payload.author: Tamala-5
raw_payload.content: Genuinely didn't enjoy it. Jurassic Rebirth fails to guarantee evolution. This cash-grab movie digs up dinosaurs only to neglect them, much like the unpolished writing and fossil record gaps. The adult cast is stuck in amber, sleepwalking through scenes, but the child actors show up with raw energy. The abrupt pacing jumps between two stories, testing patience. When dinosaurs finally appear, they seem to want to sneak off camera. This mess deserves 1.5 stars - one for the kids who acted like they cared, and half for the contractually obligated dinosaurs. Skip this and rewatch the original Jurassic Park instead.

raw_payload.author: LeonardoOliva69
raw_payload.content:
Let's face it, this franchise always triumphs despite the criticism.

I think that since the original "Jurassic Park," none of the films have received good reviews, but they always have incredible box office returns.

After the disastrous previous trilogy, where the first part lived off nostalgia and reliving moments, and "Fallen Kingdom" and "Dominion" were complete narrative disasters, it was obvious we were getting a new film.

On the one hand, there are successes: the casting is excellent, with S. Johansson, M. Ali, and J. Bailey as the main trio. There's great chemistry, and it works well. On the other hand, G. Edwards' direction knows how to work well with blockbusters.

But on the other hand, there are major omissions, a somewhat absurd script that closely mimics "Jurassic Park 3." A family, if not, that we know will survive. Few deaths, which I think would have favored the narrative and improved the atmosphere. The CGI, while good, is far from the animatronics we fell in love with. Many sequences are homages/rip-offs that many will enjoy, while others won't.

Overall, I found the film entertaining. I think they're still exaggerating the hybrids and that the trailers had shown everything, including 100% of the dinosaurs that would appear.

Unfortunately, I think the franchise is closer to "Fast and Furious" than to Michael Crichton's books, and that's a shame.

raw_payload.author: FinalSmash1689
raw_payload.content:
PROS: -The Cinematography is Spectacular!!!

-The T-Rex sequence is TERRIFYING!!!

-Heavy "Jaws" influence during the Mosasaur sequence.

-The Dinosaur effects are great.

CONS: -I'm really tired of mutants/hybrids in my Dinosaur Movies.

-Script tries to undo the fallout of "Fallen Kingdom/Dominion".

-All the characters feel like caricatures.

-The inclusion of an annoying family and a baby dino.

This was a frustrating experience. For every positive in the film, there was a corresponding negative. Gareth Edwards is always on point with cinematography and shot composition. The fact that this was shot on film was noticeable in a positive way. The visuals looked bright during the day, avoiding that blue-tinted filter most blockbusters use these days.

The Mosasaur and T-Rex sequences are actually thrilling, with the T-Rex being legitimately terrifying for the first time since "The Lost World". The effects for the dinosaurs were great, though I do miss the use of practical effects. What I don't miss is the inclusion of mutants and hybrids. I go to these movies to see dinosaurs, not monsters. There are plenty of other franchises for that. The Mutadons and D-Rex serve the same narrative purpose as the raptors (who got completely sidelined here) and the T-Rex. If they had simply swapped them out for those two in the third act, I'd have been much happier.

Also not helping were the characters. The family subplot could-and should-have been removed entirely. They add nothing to the story and waste screentime. The expedition team are all stereotypes, but Mahershala Ali at least stands out. Honestly, I think he would've been fantastic as the lead here instead of Scarlett Johansson.

All that said, I went in with no expectations and still found myself mildly entertained. I can't help but smile when the John Williams "Jurassic Park" score plays and it made a few welcome appearances here. This is definitely a 'paint-by-numbers' Jurassic movie, but I wouldn't be upset if Gareth Edwards got another chance to direct one. 7/10.

raw_payload.author: imdbfan-952067
raw_payload.content: Absolutely trash, I love the Jurassic series and this one needs to be burned and forgotten, poor plot, poor acting, it's sad that the commercials before the movie was more entertaining. I have never walked out of a movie before now.... I seriously want a refund. Everyone involved in this one should be ashamed. Don't waste your time or money. raw_payload.author: jostenson-74432
raw_payload.content:
Disappointing. I was expecting more from this for whatever reason. Here's a breakdown:-

Pros:-

Some of the scenes were good - the T-Rex scene was a standout and so was the initial Mosasaurus sequence.

Mahershala Ali - good in everything. Under used here but he still stands out from the crowd.

Cons:-

The red shirts - you know who's going to live and die from outset. Same old trope.

The corporate bad guy - again, tired trope. Money man wants to make more money. No way he would be anywhere near a dino in reality, that's what he pays people for! But we have to see him get his comeuppance of course.

The family - truly awful. Annoying kids, annoying boyfriend, a dad that needs reporting to social services. A dad that can apparently walk around on his bad leg but constantly lets his kids put themselves in danger whilst he looks on.

Mutant dinosaurs - there is no way they would have let a dinosaur that butt ugly get that big.

The rescue - get them to come and look for you in the dark because that's what you would do! Also, what helicopter pilot in their right mind would fly through smoke.

On top of all that, way too many references to other movies. The original JP, Aliens and Jaws.

It was okay, just not good enough given the hype.

raw_payload.author: imdbfan-84535
raw_payload.content: If this movie is actually the blockbuster that the media claims it is, I am shocked. This is one of the worst big-budget films I've ever seen, it is depressing to think of how much money was wasted on producing this garbage. The CGI dinosaurs look like PS4 cutscenes and are less convincing than the dinosaurs in the original Jurassic Park, which came out 32 years ago! I know this is a cliche, but I genuinely felt like the script was written by AI. Scenes sort of just happen, with nothing connecting them together. It's all just empty, soulless action with zero character development. Is it even possible to care about these characters? Somehow a family just happens to be on a boat in the middle of the ocean (???), they get attacked, and suddenly they are a part of the main cast. We'd not even gotten to know the mercenary characters, and now these random people are sloppily dumped into the story. There are pathetic attempts at comic relief (WEED IS SO FUNNY, HAHA) at completely inappropriate moments. For some reason the little girl gets a pet dinosaur because, why not? There are gigantic titanosauruses in a field that somehow are invisible when they lie down in grass. This movie is awful and I am convinced that most of the good reviews are coming from bots because I refuse to believe anyone actually "loved" this movie.

raw_payload.author: gringopaul
raw_payload.content:
If this was the first Jurassic Park movie, we would all rate it from 7 to 9. All the old tropes are there; the villains are familiar, the use of children is the same as the first one, corporate greed is represented and the dinosaurs are so inept at hunting you wonder how they survived as long as they did.

But if it were the first one, we'd all think different. The dinosaurs are the best part of the movie. The mosasaurs and spinosaurus were especially great, but the T-Rex on the river was fun, too.

Like any popcorn movie, you could tell that none of the kids would die and you could pick out which of the adults would not make it. A lot of the set pieces were borrowed from earlier films.

I rounded up. It's a 7, but people are so upset it's not at a Steven Spielberg level, there too many 1's and 2's.

raw_payload.author: stueyjrichards
raw_payload.content:
I'm genuinely confused as to how this film got made.

Zero chemistry between the main cast (which is surprising because there is some good talent here). The script is AWFUL. Every line of dialogue is purely plot driven. There are zero elements that are there to develop any character as believable.

The dinosaurs are repetitive. We see about 2 or 3 a lot. The big, scary mutated dinosaur at the end is very meh.

The family that is randomly there is the most infuriating. Who takes their children on a jaunty boat ride in the middle of dinosaur infested water? The father belongs in jail. This family add zero elements to the film and none are sympathetic. The dialogue between the boyfriend and the dad was so cringeworthy. What was their point?

Every dramatic scene just recycles everything from the first film. We have the villain driving away madly towards the docks with dinosaur DNA. We have the male hero waving the flare to save a kid. We have a kid trapped in an overturned vehicle (this time it's a boat!) and the others can't help but watch as the T Rex attacks. The original scene in the kitchen is re-created in an abandoned supermarket. There are zero new ideas here. It's time to let this franchise die.

raw_payload.author: thesar-2
raw_payload.content:
Welcome...to Steven Spielberg's Jurassic Park III.

I was ALL ready to call this movie mid yet still far superior to the last two incredibly terrible Jurassic World duds and then say, well, Fallen Kingdom and Dominion set the bar way low. I'll eat my words. This is the absolutely the best Jurassic anything since 1993's Jurassic Park started it all. By a mudslide.

YES, it has ALL the Jurassic/ancient tropes: clichés, Deus ex Machina saves, characters you absolutely know will be dino-dinners, predictability, nostalgia bait, kids in peril, the infamous "what could possibly go wrong" plans and out-of-this-world coincidences, all of which appear in the whole franchise. And DESPITE all that, this felt like thee most original one since number one. I truly had a grand time with this solid adventure.

A group of specialized people are summoned by yet another greedy, "well-meaning" corp to obtain three live samples of dinos living near the equator on another, gulp!, InGen isle. And, yes, as mentioned...what could possibly go...oh you know. The usual.

As stated in the beginning, this is all-but a retread of part III...only as if a young Spielberg directed it. Truly, this was a callback to fun, adventurous Steven's visions. And by "callback to fun, adventurous Steven's visions," I mean before his first JP sequel, The Lost World. And by "before his first JP sequel, The Lost World," I mean, in that same realm only more defined.

I'm not kidding. This was a fun time and it blew me away. Granted, I had the lowest of expectations and wasn't even planning on watching it at all after those last two painful outings. I only went to see this as an "AMC Screen Unseen" not-so-mystery movie (no detective work was needed when they announced it was PG-13 and its 2 hours 14-minute length - no other soon-to-be release matched) and its most convenient showing/time/location.

Even with low expectations and all the tropes, I felt these characters were far more real than 90% of the series. And regardless of the painful, albeit necessary character development we needed to care for them, I still did root for the ones I correctly predicted would live.

Of course, these movies should've ended with Part 2's The Lost World. Okay, maybe with Part 4's Jurassic World. I'm just glad they rebounded, rebirthed?, extremely well with Part 7.

YES, it's recommended! Definitely see it when it comes out and with the best screen/effects.

***

Final Thoughts: Here's my current ranking:

1. Jurassic Park (1993) 2. Jurassic World: Rebirth (2025) 3. The Lost World: Jurassic Park (1997) 4. Jurassic World (2015) 5. Jurassic Park III (2001) 6. Jurassic World: Dominion (2022) 7. Jurassic World: Fallen Kingdom (2018)

raw_payload.author: Darragh_Gallagher
raw_payload.content:
I'll be honest, I went into Jurassic World: Rebirth with low expectations after the disaster of Dominion. However, I was surprised with how big of an improvement it was from Dominion.

Starting with the positives, the darker and scarier aspects of the story were phenomenal. It was such a good idea to integrate tense and scary moments back into the franchise and it's actually pretty terrifying at some points. The concept of the island being where the failed mutations were left sets up a perfect opportunity for horror. To a degree it's done so well. The CGI looked really good for the majority of the film. The D-Rex was clearly cloaked by the dark and smoke to hide some of the CGI (kind of like in JP 1). There were also a number of call-backs to previous instalments which was really cool to pick up on. Just to name a couple, the gas station scene was an obvious reference to the original's kitchen scene and Duncan rolled under a closing gate just like Owen from JW. The acting was good and showed displays of genuine emotion compared to Dominion.

However, here's the issues. The biggest has to be the toning inconsistency. One minute there's a mutated monster chasing our heroes around, then in the next a character says an incredibly unfunny and corny joke. The addition of the family was a bad choice and was definitely added so there was a cute kid and a cute dinosaur to boost the ratings. The family played no role in helping except getting way more screen time than needed. I thought that they should've scrapped the family plot to make room for further exploration of the failed mutations. Had that been the prime tone and goal then it would've balanced the tone and explained more into a very interesting topic. One bonus thing is what I like to call "The T-Rex Peekaboo Moment". A girl deploys a blow up raft which blocks our view of the sleeping T-Rex and when the raft falls down a second later the T-Rex has magically teleported out of sight to make a cheap jump scare. It's comedically bad and breaks all emersion in the scene. I literally laughed out loud in the theatre when I saw this.

Overall, Jurassic World: Rebirth is a mixed bag. It's not as good as Jurassic Park or Jurassic World, but it's far better than Dominion or JP III. I really liked the concept of the film being mutated failed dinosaur creations. Had some parts been removed and replaced, it would've made such a difference. Jurassic World: Rebirth is by no means a bad movie and it's actually a lot of fun on first watch. I highly recommend you go and watch it.

Worst Jurassic Park by far. Absolutely terrible movie. Small very predictable spoilers included.

First off they give us no reason to like the characters at all. They are poorly written and you spend the first half of the movie wondering if they are the good guys or bad guys. This absolutely ruins the suspense or any scare factor because were not sure if were even supposed to like them. Scarlet Johansens character is only in it for money the whole time and is supposed to be this mercenary soldier person but when it comes down to it she can't hit the broad side of a barn with any weapon they give, or she's screaming for help.

The CGI is abundant and terribly done. There is a small cargo plane pulling 6g turns for no reason, it looks like a cartoon. It seems like they had no plan when shooting the scenes with the real actors. Often times they are acting in the wrong direction or not reacting to things happening on screen. I swear the CGI team has never even seen a boat before. All the of the boat scenes dont make sense. From the "crew" just running around randomly to the guy that understands english but only speaks french the whole movie, to the listing of the boat being completely inconsistent. The boat gets hit by a dinosaur at one point and none of the actors even move. It gets bumped on the hull and for some unexplained reason it then starts pluming thick black smoke from somewhere else unexplained. What was even damaged? Then with litteraly an entire ocean to escape in they decide to drive right into the sharp rocks?

There are weird things that happen seemingly for no reason, like needing to fire a rifle from the very exposed extended bow of the boat while being roped into it when they could have just fired from the safety of the deck. A guy in a facility where everyone is wearing tyvex suits and masks and eye protection chowing a snickers and dropping the wrapper on the ground, then it gets sucked into an intake vent on a door? Why the F is there an intake vent on door? And why is it on the ground?? Then that snickers wrapper causes the door to fail which then causes the entire security and computer system to fail, releasing all the dinosaurs. I mean come on. That is even lazier writing that "somehow Palpetine returned". Its like they think we're stupid.

There are literally only 8 types of dinosaurs in this movie. 8. And for some reason they all give up on eating everyone after like 2 minutes of trying.

This movie has more plot holes that ive ever seen in one movie. At one point a T-rex picks up and inflatable raft and crushes it, only to put it back down and it doesn't have any marks. Like not even a scratch, AND it's still perfectly inflated. Then they use the raft to PADDLE away slowly from it. AND IT JUST GIVES UP! What happened to T-Rex's being intelligent hunters like in the original trilogy?

There is ZERO continuity in this film. One character badly hurts his leg and needs a cane to walk then suddenly can carry a child and run? His limp completely disappears half way through the movie. Keep in mind this movie takes place over 2 days.

There is a scene where one character has a gun and runs out of ammo, then proceeds to act as if it is still loaded, then in the next scene the gun is gone!

There is a guy with his hand handcuffed to a brief case and suddenly the handcuff disappears when the other characters need to grab it. Like not even a broken chain or broken handcuff. Its just not there anymore.

Spoilers after this part: The main big bad dinosaur is basically a rip off of the rancor in Star Wars Return of the Jedi. And it also gives up after 2 minutes and seeing a flare. One of the characters sacrifices himself by leading the dino away with a flare, and get this, SWIMMING. He gets away SWIMMING... WITH A FLARE IN HIS HAND. HOW DOES HE NOT GET EATEN??? Then somehow he gets away and shoots another flare so he can be rescued, but the dino now suddenly doesnt care about flares?

In conclusion, it is a terrible movie. I want my 2 and a half hours back.

raw_payload.author: ferguson-6
raw_payload.content:
Greetings again from the darkness. It's been 32 years since John Hammond "spared no expense" on ice cream and Chaos Theory Doctor Malcolm pleaded that the colorful jeep "must go faster". That 1993 film by Steven Spielberg awed us and changed the course of cinema. Now director Gareth Edwards (ROGUE ONE: A STAR WARS STORY, 2016; GODZILLA, 2014) brings us the seventh film in the 'Jurassic' franchise. Having adapted Michael Crichton's books for the first two in the series, renowned screenwriter David Koepp (who has also contributed to Mission: Impossible, Indiana Jones, and Spider-Man franchises, among others) returns for "Rebirth".

Opening with a flashback to fifteen years prior, we see the InGen lab where experimental cross-breeding has resulted in dino-mutations, and we are only about four minutes in when we get the first dino snack (an unfortunate lab worker). Returning to modern day times (five years after the end of JURASSIC WORLD: DOMINION), we find a citizenry somehow bored with the dinosaurs that have been released into the wild - most of which have died or are dying due to environmental issues caused by us despicable humans. The exception is a remote island near the equator where the climate most resembles prehistoric times, and the beasts are free to roam ... as it's now a "No people allowed" sanctuary. It's also a former InGen lab site.

This all sets the stage for greedy Big Pharma executive, Martin Krebs (Rupert Friend) to plan an excursion with the goal of collecting DNA from the three largest dino species from land, sea, and air. Oh, and minor detail - the DNA has to be collected from live specimens. The DNA can supposedly be used for major medical breakthroughs. Krebs hires Zora Bennett (Scarlett Johansson), a mercenary for "situational security and reaction", and Dr Henry Loomis (Jonathan Bailey, WICKED, 2024) a paleontologist trained by the infamous Dr Grant. Zora assembles the rest of the team, including friend and boat captain Duncan Kincaid (Oscar winner Mahershala Ali), and the rest of his crew.

Separately, a family sailing the same waters is introduced. Reuben (Manuel Garcia-Rulfo) and his two daughters, college-aged Teresa (Luna Blaise) and clever 11-year-old Isabella (Audrina Miranda), along with Teresa's lazy and spacey boyfriend Xavier (David Iacono).

This secondary group provides us with 'normal' folks to fear for as their boat is dino-attacked, leaving them stranded in the sea, awaiting recovery by the pharma boat. This creates a strange mixture of folks and, most importantly, allows for the mandatory kid-in-peril. Another dino-attack on the boat separates the family from the team and begins the process of scratching characters from the story.

The adventures are quite different for the two groups, and the island scenery is consistently awe-inspiring. But we are all here for dinosaurs, and on that front, the film never disappoints. Very few gaps occur between sightings or attacks, and the dinosaurs dazzle. A slumbering by the river T-Rex provides one of the best chase/attack scenes from any of the Jurassic movies (with a tip of the raft to the Crichton novel). Of course, we know who the villain will be, and we know how things go for villains in these movies. The story includes an altruistic approach from Loomis, who flashes some survival instincts despite a career spent in an air-conditioned museum, rather than a dino-filled jungle.

We are left to ponder what the titular "Rebirth" refers to. The film doesn't offer anything new, outside of the mutants ... including the much-anticipated D-Rex (sporting the head of a Beluga whale?). Your individual level of critique will determine whether you buy Scarlett Johansson as the ultimate tough warrior, although John Mathieson's stunning cinematography and composer Alexandre Desplat's score (generously sprinkled with John Williams' iconic JP score) are complementary to an overall entertaining and crowd-pleasing big-budget summer blockbuster, with many nods to the previous films in the franchise. Race cars and dinosaurs in the same summer ... spared no expense.

Opens in theaters on July 2, 2025.

raw_payload.author: pjzppmgh
raw_payload.content:
I've been a fan of the franchise since the beginning. Jurassic Park was one of the best movies of all time and still holds up today. JP2 and even 3 were good additions to the franchise. Jurassic World was a well-done if a bit flawed "soft reboot" of the franchise after a long wait, although the other 2 in the World series have not been nearly as good as any of the predecessors.

I did enjoy Rebirth, for the most part. I liked how they scaled back the presence of the dinosaurs in the modern world and at least partially addressed the fact that the environment in which dinosaurs actually lived was very different from our modern environment. I thought this was another good "soft reboot" that helped focus the action a bit more.

The lead cast of Scarlett Johansen, Mahershala Ali, and the incomparable Jonathan Bailey did well, and they had pretty decent chemistry between them. I could see watching and enjoying another couple of movies with these characters.

But honestly the absolute worst thing about this movie was the family. There's the father, who thinks it's a good idea to sail straight through the equatorial region which has become the home to the dinosaurs. The youngest daughter, who is just incredibly irritating. And tangentially the storyline about her deciding to take home a baby dinosaur in spite of the fact that it's well-established that the dinosaurs can't really survive outside the equatorial regions. Then there's the oldest daughter, who is nearly as irritating as her sister and is also obviously an idiot given that she's dating the absolute loser that is her boyfriend. And the boyfriend, who starts off as a loser and somehow manages to get even worse as the film progresses. I was literally rooting for the Mosasaur in the first encounter.

If they had ditched the family, this would've been a much more enjoyable movie. And if they do decide to make more movies, I hope to God the family is absent. Or at least that they get eaten quickly.

What began as a groundbreaking franchise back in '93 has sadly turned into a goofy, plotless mess by 2025. Jurassic Park was actually the very first movie I ever saw in a theater, so it's always held a special place in my heart. And even though none of the sequels ever truly lived up to the original, I still made sure to watch every single one. But this last one? It's in a league of its own-just for all the wrong reasons.

First off, how do you end up with worse graphics than a movie that came out 32 years ago? That completely blows my mind. The original's visuals were stunning and still hold up today, yet this one looked and felt like a bad video game from start to finish.

And while I know it's not entirely fair to keep comparing everything to the first film, the difference in characters is hard to ignore. The original had a brilliant "squad": two paleontologists, a chaotician, a lawyer, and two kids-one of whom actually knew a lot about dinosaurs. This time? We got a group of people who seemed to know nothing about dinosaurs, security, or... well, anything really.

The whole movie just felt like an overlong escape room full of shouting, screaming, and clumsy, almost cartoonish dinosaurs that didn't even seem to know how to hunt properly. It was disappointing, to say the least.

raw_payload.author: bankofmarquis
raw_payload.content:
And now...a summer blockbuster film from the "no one asked for it" department.

It's hard to believe that the original JURASSIC PARK film brought ''shock and awe" CGI dinosaurs to the big screen 32 years ago. This Stephen Spielberg blockbuster brought in the age (good and bad) of giant CGI summer films. This led to the inevitable trilogy that were still interesting and fun...but definitely followed the law of diminishing returns.

And then...just when you thought you'd seen the last of the CGI dinosaurs of the Jurassic period, the retro/nostalgic JURASSIC WORLD burst onto the scene in 2015 and brought another trilogy of diminishing returns to the screen. And when the final installment (JURASSIC WORLD: DOMINION) hit the screens with a thud, one could safely assume that we had seen the last of this series.

But...the suits at Universal noticed one thing - over $2.2 billion in Worldwide Box Office for the series.

So...whether we like it or not...we get another installment of "humans getting eaten by dinosaurs" - JURASSIC WORLD: REBIRTH where Scarlett Johansson (BLACK WIDOW) leads yet another group of human food onto yet ANOTHER island that INGEN was using for dinosaur genetic manipulation.

This Gareth Edwards (the best Star Wars film, ROGUE ONE) effort has quite a bit going for it...an interesting (enough) premise that is well (and simply) laid out, engaging stars in Johansson, Jonathan Bailey (Fiero in WICKED) and 2x Academy Award winner Mahershala Ali (GREENBOOK) who all earn their paychecks trying to bring some depth to thinly written characters who's main role is to look up in "shock and awe" at CGI dinosaurs and CGI that REALLY, REALLY works (for the most part), especially a water attack on a boat.

And...there is a young girl in peril, Audrina Miranda (in what looks to be her Major Film debut) who makes a pet of a young dino that is "just so cute", but Edwards knows how to pull back just in time to keep it from getting annoying.

However...this film commits many critical errors:

1). It gives work to the worst actor in the business today, Ed Skrein (MIDWAY), who comes clamoring onto the screen with such a one-note performance that he might as well wear an "eat me" sign on his back.

2). Unfortunately, that performance isn't the worst one of the film. Rupert Friend (COMPANION) plays the villain of the piece that does everything but twirl his mustache and tie Johansson to the railroad tracks.

3). The CGI in this film is great...EXCEPT...for a scene towards the end of the film where they either ran out of time, money or interest (probably all 3). My kids can make a better CGI scene on their phones than what was foisted on us at the end.

4). Everyone in this film is WAY TOO PRETTY! The couldn't throw a bone to "weathered" character actors like Danny Trejo, Dale Dickey or even good ol' Shea Wigham? Nope. They broke open the Ford Modeling Agency books and said..."if they look good, they probably will taste good to the dinosaurs".

5). NONE of the main characters are worth rooting for (I'm looking at you Johannson and Bailey), though Ali tries really, really hard to give us some sort of better character than the weakly written character he was given by veteran screenwriter David Koepp (let's go with the Tom Cruise THE MUMMY), while a quartet of the players in the "B" story are much more interesting and worth our while. Here's an idea, let's do a film focusing solely on these characters!

And, finally, this film committed the CARDINAL SIN of these types of movies: the dinosaur-to-humans getting eaten ratio is waaaaaay too low!

So, come for some very good dinosaur CGI, but you'll have to wade through all the sins of the film to find the few gems.

Letter Grade: B-

6 stars (out of 10) and you can take that to the Bank (ofMarquis)

raw_payload.author: LeowanK

raw_payload.content:
Overall I had a really good time with Jurassic World Rebirth, i went in with tempered expectations, and they just about matched it. It's a flawed adventure where you can probably nitpick it to death, but it also offers so much good that you can overlook some of those problems. It has genuinely nail biting scenes, looks beautiful, introduces some really fun characters, and has some of the best music the franchise has seen for over 20 years.

It definitely feels like just another Jurassic Park sequel, its not trying to be the next Endgame with its scale, its just trying to be a good standalone adventure, and I think it does the job well.

I wish Gareth Edwards was given more time to perfect the film, since he wasn't given much time pre and post production to make sure everything is as good as it can be, but theres no other director i could imagine delivering something as high quality as this in that space of time.

Definitely recommend.

raw_payload.author: mattgflan
raw_payload.content:
As a lifelong fan of Jurassic Park movies this was a major let down.

This was terrible. The plot is ALL OVER THE PLACE. We're supposed to believe after 30 years, the entire world is sick and tired of dinosaurs. They shoehorned in every single trope, most of them didn't fit or got all of 2 sentences for development, and the dinosaurs didn't even do much.

There's a random family in this movie that didn't have to be there. (Why they're in illegal waters known to have dinosaurs in it is beyond me)

There are so many little things that just make no sense, too many to list.

I wish the resolve to make a good Jurassic Park movie was as strong as the raft they use in the middle of the movie.

raw_payload.author: Gbp127272
raw_payload.content:
Really enjoyed it. The raft scene stole the show for me. Most of the characters were good.

I swear the major things most movies are missing these days are a solid all the way through sound score. Some of the more tense Dino scenes would've benefited greatly if they had removed the canned action background music and replaced it with more a slow tense build the drama/fear score. Sometimes let's just hear the waterfall in the background or the trees in the wind. Silence can be a great suspense builder.

Still some problems here and there with CGI. I always prefer real props as much as possible.

Overall though very pleased with this addition.

raw_payload.author: laceup-96849
raw_payload.content: Honestly looking at the reviews here I expected as usual jurassic movie, same old dinosaurs running around, obviously it was predictable but surprisingly it was much better than expected, yah dinosaurs did some weird stuffs, i mean its a movie for gods sake, its supposed to be entertaining, so they definitely took the liberty in doing it, the story had a very emotional connection to the characters, the Casting was great, the small kid in the movie did bring life into the movie, Mahershala definitely was as usual great, its a movie you can watch with your family, so do check it out if possible,

raw_payload.author: SimCinSpirit
raw_payload.content:
Spielberg and Co have betrayed Jurassic Park fans with a pile of dino dung so huge Ian Malcolm would call it a statistical anomaly.

I hated this film so much. The writing was excruciatingly bad, with none of the characters worth saving (except Ali and the dumb bf, who was funny). Acting was completely meh for the big stars.

* Spoilers *

Yachting trips on the open ocean are dangerous enough without a solid crew. Why would a father take his two daughters and stoner bf on a yachting trip through monster-infested waters?

Scarlett Johansson isn't plausible as a security specialist, and her character is both dull and incompetent. She basically leads everyone to their deaths with her "I'll take care of ya... maybe" attitude. What was she even good for-the one scene at the end when she shot the bird-raptor? Otherwise, 'twas the blind leading the blind on this one. The little girl did more to help the group, and she was annoying as well.

The museum guy was an absolute bore and a twit, yet he still managed to navigate the island and locate all the dinos as if he'd done it before. I wish he'd been eaten instead of poor Frenchy (one of those twit characters who understands English but only answers in French).

The scene where Scarlett's character and Mahershala Ali's talk about their exes was so dull and cringey that my friends and I laughed out loud. Who cares about past lovers who never appear and have zero significance, apart from a lazy writer's attempt to add dimension to these greedy, stupid mercenaries?

The father lets his young daughter focus on a pet dinosaur instead of situational awareness. The older daughter inflates a raft right in front of a T-rex. The bf wanders a mile away from everyone in dinosaur territory to take a leak. They stroll through a dino-infested jungle chatting like it's a school field trip.

The mutant monster dinosaurs. Yawn, what a bore. Why did they include them, why? Dinosaurs are the most amazing "real" monsters ever, and they had to create "the ugliest, meanest ones" thinking it'll add to the drama and tension? The mutants pale in comparison to the scenes of the original 1993 T-Rex and raptors.

And on and on and on-this movie sucks. So did all the other Jurassic World movies. Shame on you, Spielberg and Co. Shame on you.

raw_payload.author: Brentjwestern
raw_payload.content: The first line of the movie was something like "Why do we keep doing this" and was related to the movie content. The same could be said of these movies. I grew up on these and love the idea. But we have to reel these in and make them worthwhile. No more cash grabs. They used to be spectacular, now we just hope they aren't bad.

raw_payload.author: Benjamin-3042

Also there was no battles like when the D Rex came I was expecting a T. Rex and a ton of dinosaurs to gang up on the D Rex but no.

And also how did the boyfriend survive like there was over 100 gallons of water blocking the door and somehow broke through and when he got hit by the sail and just popped up like nothing happened?

Come on Jurassic Park I Waited for 3 years to see this movie man I was hyped and during the end I almost fell asleep because of how bad it was maybe some people will like it but most won't please make a another movie but add an actual fight scene.

raw_payload.author: atribecalledmike
raw_payload.content: This is not a knock! I'm a sucker for the third Jurassic Park movie, for its simplicity and straightforward plot. Hell, I like it better than the second installment. This movie is very similar in plot line and sophistication... or lack there of. It's not going to blow you away with a great script, huge surprises or amazing acting. Certain choices are absolutely ridiculous. But at the end of the day, it's still a really entertaining movie if you allow it to be. Just relax, sit back, turn your brain off for a couple of hours and it will be just fine.

raw_payload.author: VictorM-358
raw_payload.content:
Absolutely a waste of time! 20 scenes running from a dinosaurs that could have snatched them 15 times untill he actually does to eventually not eat them and move on!

There is absolutely no story line. And the creators of the movie made absolutely no effort to try to make the science in the movie anywhere near "possible". Not even "movie possible"!

You would need a island the size of Australia to sustain a population of dinosaur, and that size of animals, that big. And nobody on earth has ever known about this island.

I almost walked out the cinema but my girlfriend made me stay! I want my money and time back!

raw_payload.author: elijaharredondo
raw_payload.content:
Jurassic World: Rebirth is the sequel we've been waiting for since 1993. It brilliantly returns to the tone & atmosphere of the original Jurassic Park, where dinosaurs feel like animals again, not monsters. The film strips away the glossy overkill of the last few installments & rebuilds with tension, wonder, & grounded horror.

The new D-Rex is absolutely terrifying, finally bringing to life the scrapped hybrid concept teased from the original JP4 drafts. It's a menacing, intelligently-used threat that doesn't feel over the top, but deeply unsettling. The raptors are back to being cold-blooded predators, unpredictable & primal, just as they should be.

Scarlett delivers a fantastic performance, grounding the human element with a blend of empathy & grit. The river sequence in particular is iconic, a suspenseful, beautifully shot set piece that captures the awe & danger Spielberg's original was known for.

This isn't just fan service, it's payoff. For longtime fans, Rebirth feels like a respectful evolution that finally gives us what we've been asking for: dinosaurs as animals, not movie monsters. It's thrilling, nostalgic, and surprisingly restrained in all the right ways.

raw_payload.author: ThereelscoopwithKK
raw_payload.content:
What the Jurassic Park/ World franchise struggles with the most is that it's so repetitive that you could probably show the same movie every year and people might not even recognize it as the same one. However, the movies consistently make bank , so the question is why would they ever go away from this model.

One thing I will say is that I do believe this "rebirth" (reboot) of sorts does make some improvements. Chris Pratt's time with the franchise was certainly complete , and Scarlett Johansson is a nice fill in as the star lead. The story is structured better overall and there are some fairly good quality action scenes as well as some shots that look beautiful on the screen. The parts with the dinosaurs for the most part are significantly improved than the last couple installments. Still, the ending drags on longer than it needs to leaving you with a tired feeling instead of leaving you with an energized invigorating feeling like many of the earlier beautiful scenes with the dinos.

What this movie gets very wrong though is its characters. Zora Bennett (Scarlet Johansson) and Duncan Kinkaid ( Maheshala Ali) are the only characters that perform well enough to make you believe their characters at all .

Even still when the film is trying to build the characters early on it feels like lots of time passes but you aren't really developing any deeper feeling or knowledge about them. A lot of one liners are thrown in that hint at a characters past but never enough is told to understand their deeper passions or ambitions.

I'm also not sure the addition of the random family traveling at sea that they rescued needed to be a part of the film. The script and inclusion of the "lazy" boyfriend is especially clunky. I'd have preferred if we had got more time with our main cast than had to split it between both storylines.

At the end of the day , there won't be many people calling this their favourite movie , but they will still be back for next year's installment. As a summer movie it fills a role. As much as I enjoyed some of the almost blissful scenes with the dinosaurs there's still too many problems going on to give it a higher score.

raw_payload.author: juasonason
raw_payload.content:
I can't think of a single redeeming quality of this movie, and can't believe execs at the top gave it the green light to move forward. This is evidence that Hollywood is incapable of making even a decent movie anymore. Making a good movie requires actual work, passion and talent, which is clearly not what anyone who had anything to do with this movie had when it was made. This is especially true for the writers and director, who, at this point are has-beens who don't know how to make a good story.

Every single scene takes the "suspension of disbelief" up to a ten and requires you to just completely shut off your brain. Parts that were supposed to be comic relief were just awkward. If I had had rotten tomatoes with me, I would have thrown them at the screen. Watching them slowly ooze and drip off of the screen would have been more entertainment than what this trash of a movie was.

Seriously, those who give this anything above a 1 star rating is why Hollywood continues to roll out hot, wet garbage, like this, year after year.

raw_payload.author: JPFLIP
raw_payload.content: Cannot use 27.405 as a rescue frequency. This is a CB channel and has limited range so rescue would no be possible at the location where they are. Inland it maybe possible, but not that far out at sea. The boat should have been equipped with a HF marine radio. Any good boat captain would know this...

raw_payload.author: Venus_Grace
raw_payload.content:
I can't help but be critical of this film's narration, character introductions and personal stakes. The dialogue is stiff and contrived, the storytelling is abysmal, and the acting could have been better.

Jonathan Bailey and Mahershala Ali were the only two castings that delivered.

Disappointing for a Steven Spielberg production, and vexing to an aspiring filmmaker who can spot every single aspect that could have been improved.

If you want a little mindless thrill/some jump scares, you'll enjoy this. If good storytelling is important to you, I don't think this film is for you.

raw_payload.author: Muhammad29
raw_payload.content: This film is a thrilling, visually stunning spectacle that'll leave you breathless! Gareth Edwards masterfully crafts a Spielberg-esque adventure, blending nostalgic nods to Jurassic Park with heart-pumping action sequences and awe-inspiring dinosaurs. Finally! The T-Rex river raft scene saw the light of day! The new creatures are both majestic and menacing, dude that T-Rex was big, making for an unforgettable experience. With standout performances from the cast, including Jonathan Bailey bringing his signature charm and Scarlett Johansson delivering intensity, this movie is a must-see. Edwards' understanding of dinosaurs as both majestic and terrifying shines through, making this a hugely impressive film. The film's visuals are breathtaking, and the anxiety-filled action sequences will have you on the edge of your seat. However, be warned: intense dismemberment and gore might spook some viewers. If you're a fan of the Jurassic series, you'll love it - and hopefully, Edwards will continue to bring these prehistoric wonders to life! With its perfect blend of nostalgia, thrills, and stunning visuals, this film is an absolute must-watch on the big screen.

raw_payload.author: louieshowers
raw_payload.content:
This was even worse than the last one. Totally unrealistic in every way imaginable, even for a movie about dinosaurs roaming the earth today.

Slow, disjointed, with injuries that appear and disappear from scene to scene (a broken leg). Absolutely no storyline whatsoever. The dinosaurs looked weird and seemed to have superhuman power. Don't waste your time watching this.

Another annoying aspect of the movie was the melodramatic facial expressions and zooming in on the drama. Characters were cliche and just unlikeable. I mean the only positive quality about that movie was the scenery. No 👎🏼👎🏼 thanks.

raw_payload.author: ellamariap

raw_payload.author: GrantF-20
raw_payload.content: In the words of Malcolm "that is one big pile of sh*". Scarlett crying over an ex or something, Bailey trying to give the motive meaning. They have chemistry which is good, considering the limited script and storytelling. Any movie that starts with writing is usually not a good sign. The movie is Jaws, Dora the Explorer and Tomb Raider mash up with a few nods and camera angles to Jurassic Park. The T-Rex scene was its only saving grace but still doesn't hold the suspense very long. The VFX were terrible, some of the worst in the franchise. Blue screens everywhere, the "locations" even look fake. They should let this franchise go extinct.

raw_payload.author: Coventry
raw_payload.content:
The previous one, "Dominion" was a complete disaster, and - no - I'm not writing that because I'm an old & embittered guy who only likes the 1993 original. I quite liked the two "World" sequels from 2015 and 2018. The saga should have ended with "Fallen Kingdom", but writer/director Colin Treverrow absolutely wanted to complete his own trilogy and added prehistoric locusts and a lame attempt to bring back Sam Neill and Laura Dern. Recycling the stars of the original landmark is always a sign of a decaying franchise.

Enter Gareth "Godzilla" Edwards, whose approach for the 7th instalment of the Jurassic series is much smarter and more effective. And, most of all, more entertaining! It's back to blockbuster mode, with a straightforward plot and stereotype characters, exotic locations, love & astonishment for the dinos, spectacular action footage and people-eating! The plot first explains that practically all dinosaurs are extinct once more, due to the climate changes and the inability to coexist with humans. Good, so we don't have to deal with that bizarre situation again. Dinos only survive in regions around the equator, and that is exactly where the sneaky corporate rat Martin Krebs wants to send an expedition to. For the development of a groundbreaking new medicine against heart diseases, Krebs' pharma company needs blood samples from the biggest dinosaur's species that ever lived respectively in the sea, on land, and in the air. Now, that's what I call a blockbuster plot! If you then also cast Scarlett Johansson as a tough mercenary gal, your return on investment is practically guaranteed!

Evidently, there's a lot to whine and complain about as well. David Koepp's script hangs together with cliches and derivative situations. How odd that the well-trained mercenaries die one by one, while the lovable family - who were just out sailing in the middle of the ocean - miraculously survive attacks from a Mosasaurus and a T-Rex. Oh well, at least "Renaissance" (which is how it was released in my country instead of "Rebirth") treated my family and me two hours of unpretentious excitement at the cinema, and that was all I hoped for.

raw_payload.author: K177Y_K47
raw_payload.content:
This movie barely has anything to do with the Jurassic franchise we know and love. Instead, we get slammed with pointless family drama-a disrespectful boyfriend, a dad who might as well be invisible, and a teenage daughter loudly airing out her romantic life in front of her little sister. Teenagers spilling their romantic life in front of a kid and casually offering weed to a parent - in a Jurassic movie. Somewhere between the dinos and the drama, good storytelling went extinct.

The film would've been 80% better if they scrapped the family subplot and focused on the actual mission. There was real potential to expand the Jurassic universe in a bold, fresh direction-but instead, we got a disjointed mess with dinosaurs sprinkled in the background like an afterthought. Major missed opportunity.

raw_payload.author: frey-rebecca27
raw_payload.content: I had the pleasure of seeing this as part of Secret Movie Club, and what a fun surprise! You can tell the actors and actresses were so excited to be apart of this film, and it was just good old fashioned fun. I felt the nostalgia and joy in this and come on folks you know why you are watching this sort of film. It ignites that inner child and wonder it would be to walk on an earth where dinosaurs live. I loved it! Give this a chance and know walking in that you will be entertained and feel the movies magic. Lastly, there was a part of me like "great another Jurassic park movie" but boy did it turn my cynical view around. Go see this and enjoy!

raw_payload.author: Sam56800
raw_payload.content: It's slightly better than the Jurassic World trilogy (which isn't saying much because that trilogy was terrible). I appreciate the story of people wanting dino DNA instead of simply hunting them. But everything else was pretty bad - acting, writing, and most importantly, visual effects. People go to these movies for the spectacle and effects, so failing that is pretty much just missing on the main selling point for these movies. Every time I see a dino, I notice the green screen effect. The perspectives are all off, and it feels like they're staring into a flat wall that's later added with effects. The dinos don't feel like they have weight and momentum, and there's no sense they're actually there, which takes you out of the movie. The only decent scene was the t-rex one, and the only shots where it looked good was when it was alone, with no humans around. As a stand-alone prop, it looked alright. But as soon as you add people to the scene, the fakeness of it becomes apparent. A far, far departure from Jurassic Park 1 and 2, which even to this day, feels way more real than any movie that's been done.

raw_payload.author: yourlittletsun
raw_payload.content:
If this movie is not a cashgrab, then it is just a pure insult to the original Jurassic Park movies. When you thought that Jurassic World: Dominion was bad, this was way worse than that.

The story started because a pharmacy company wanted to create a medicine from dinosaur blood. So, they sent a group of highly trained people to get the samples in a said dangerous island. The stupidiest part about this is that NO ONE except one person brought a gun in this mission. Somehow, the group magically survived the whole journey in an island full of dinosaurs. Amazing.

Meanwhile, there was a family who got themselves into this trouble. It was ridiculous to see how they survived until the very end considering they also had zero weapons nor training. The plot armor was thicker than a dinosaur skin. Not to mention how annoying some of these characters were. I'm so mad they kept the boyfriend alive.

At the final act, everyone reunited at this abandoned facility where they encountered D-rex. The concept of mutated dinosaur was cool, but there was no in depth explanation to it. It felt flat and boring. Not even a dinosaur vs dinosaur fight scene. Then, they left the island with a boat raft. So peaceful that it was unbelievable considering how they just encountered tons of sea dinosaurs at the beginning of the movie. The movie ended just like that. No scene of what they did with the blood samples, but I just assumed they made a medicine accessible for everyone.

To conclude, I hated how they titled this movie. What was rebirthed here? The same old character tropes and failing plot with no meaning and depth? Please, do not waste time on this movie. It's not worth it.

raw_payload.author: marquimedes-81023
raw_payload.content:
It's insulting how bad the script is. Every single decision, with very few exceptions is an offense to intelligence.

If there is danger on the water, they go. If there is danger on land, that becomes their target. If there is an easy way to do things, why take it.

It's difficult not to dwell on the inconsistencies of the movie, from basic common sense to poorly fitted CGI, including all the canonical repertoire of shallow characters and troupes.

I pirated the movie and I still feel scammed.

raw_payload.author: KirstenJ-4
raw_payload.content:
If your kids have seen the other Jurassic Park/World movies, they will be fine. Do people get eaten whole? Yes. Are there intense scenes? Of course. And the D-Rex is more of a monster than a dinosaur (imagine a beluga whale mixed with Alien). Are the descriptions in the parents' guide accurate? Yes-ish... nothing was as horrifying or graphic as those comments made it out to be. Our family's experience was that it was less gory and violent than the Jurassic World trilogy, and moreso than the Jurassic Park trilogy.

Is this our new favorite Jurassic movie? No. But we liked it. We were entertained, and it was a fun family night out.

raw_payload.author: RichardVella_
raw_payload.content:
Jurassic World Rebirth is a solid, fun ride that treats us with great action/suspenseful scenes (which reminded me of Jaws) and some humour. One thing that stood out is the cinematography, and the dinosaurs actually act more like animals rather than monsters.

The cast was good together, with Mahersala Ali being the standout. The movie started slowly and a bit messy, but picked up as it got closer to the island, and I don't think the side family was needed, but it provided some humour. Although they probably had the best scene with T-Rex. I would have loved more scenes of the D-Rex! It felt like we only had 3 minutes of screen time, but we could have had way more with it. It's definitely terrifying. It's not a perfect film, but it's still great (much better than the last two!).

raw_payload.author: BrnyA
raw_payload.content:
I went into Jurassic World with low expectations - and while it wasn't quite as terrible as I feared, it still failed to stand on its own. The film rehashes iconic moments from Jurassic Park (like the raptor kitchen scene) without adding anything fresh. Nostalgia is used lazily, and most of the story is built on recycled ideas and shallow characters.

Scientifically, it's absurd. From a doctor extracting DNA from a quetzalcoatlus egg white to inconsistent creature sizes and pseudoscientific nonsense, the film doesn't even try to be believable. The characters are empty, the dialogues forced, and the emotional impact nonexistent - though, oddly enough, one baby's death scene drew laughter instead of sympathy.

That said, some scenes were genuinely gripping: Leclerc's self-sacrifice was one of the most brutal in the series, and the jungle sequences had real atmosphere. The Mosasaurus moments, in particular, stood out for their intensity and visuals. The sound design was solid, and the CGI looked good - although sadly, no animatronics were used, which made the film feel even more artificial.

In the end, it's a forgettable summer blockbuster. Hardcore fans and those familiar with the Jurassic lore will likely find it painful, while casual viewers might enjoy it as a mindless creature feature. The franchise deserves better, and it's still not too late to fix it, but only if Hollywood stops prioritizing profit over quality.

Final rating: 2 out of 10 Snickers wrappers.

Just like the D-rex: it should never have been made, and it's time to put it to rest.

raw_payload.author: arvinjanasis
raw_payload.content:
It's been 3 years since the last Jurassic film was released and now we have another! This film is a mix of a mission and a trip that lead to survival. It showed how dinos are truly predators and are truly dangerous and not some pets. Only gripe (without mentioning spoilers) is the fact that it could have had more dino action but overall it was good. The character side actually made sense with a group going to the island for a mission while a civilian group just getting to a normal vacation was attacked by spinos and a mososaur. The score was amazing as they played the original theme couple of times playing homage to John Williams! Was kind of distracted to Scarlett Johansson too but if they could have made this scarier, it would have been loads better.

All because of a snicker bar.
