raw_payload.author: dandare4479
raw_payload.content: A good thrill ride of great Dino set pieces. This film shares alot with the concept of the lost world Jurassic park. While not quite as dark as that film, this one is darker than the three previous jurassic world movies, and for the better. The added realism creates great tension during several great set pieces in the middle section of the film. It is a shame it is let down by some poorly written characters, plot, and the usual unrealistic survivals. The ending section could of been better in creating more tension for the characters. However it's a solid scary family blockbuster. Arguably the best since the original.

raw_payload.author: valestra-90591
raw_payload.content: There is no story, no tension, no magic, a redundant, paste and copy from the other movies with no soul or character development of any kind. I feel nothing for these characters and matter of fact the family addition to the story is so dumb that at a certain point you start to root for the t-rex. How can you spend 180 million and come out with this below par of a product? This is some of the worst i have seen from this franchise, i get Dino are fascinating but this is not it, feels like they have put the least amount of effort just to get another 600 millions out of the brand. I have no idea how are people giving higher scores to this generic cash grab. The audience deserves more.

raw_payload.author: 4170123W
raw_payload.content:
Jurassic World: Rebirth delivers exactly what longtime fans of the franchise have come to expect - high-stakes action, chaotic dinosaur chases, and enough explosions to shake a theater seat. It doesn't reinvent the wheel, but it leans confidently into its formula, and for the most part, it works.

What surprised me most is how much I actually enjoyed it. While the story is nothing groundbreaking (and at times predictable), the execution is solid. The film features some truly impressive set pieces - large-scale sequences that are visually stunning and tightly edited. A few moments even dip into genuine horror territory, creating tension and dread in ways the previous films rarely attempted.

The characters are serviceable, though none truly stand out, and some emotional beats feel a little forced. Still, the film knows its audience. It's here to entertain - and it does just that. As popcorn blockbusters go, Rebirth delivers a fun ride with plenty of dino mayhem. Don't expect depth, but if you go in looking for spectacle and chaos, you won't be disappointed.

raw_payload.author: Rene_W_Feuerlein
raw_payload.content: I was 29 when the first Jurassic Park movie came out, and I've been a fan of the franchise ever since. I was so looking forward to another installment. I saw this one on the premiere day July 2nd with my son and some of his friends, and all but one was disappointed. The first third, perhaps even half of the movie, I found totally non-engaging, and had I not been with my son I might even have walked out, so to watch it in my home theatre setup when it later was released on disc. Especially I was not impressed by Scarlett Johansson's performance, and most of the other characters. By comparison I found Chris Pratt's and Bryce Dallas Howard's performance in the previous three movies, and off course Sam Neil, Jeff Goldblum, and Laura Dern an order of magnitude better! Actually I find any of the other six Jurassic Park movies much better! It is quite possible this installment would better please someone, who was not imprinted in their youth by the first three movies, and perhaps even did not see the three sequels. Sure I'll watch again when it released for home use, but I'm not anticipating like an eager Beaver!

raw_payload.author: oursystems
raw_payload.content:
I wasn't expecting a great deal going into this. A few characters that could generate some vicarious sympathy, a plot twist or two, some well-crafted cliff-hanger moments and a bit of humorous relief -- that would have done it. This type of film is easy to enjoy if reasonably well executed.

Sadly, this film delivers NONE of the essentials. After two interminable hours, all of the characters are exactly what they started out as: stock cardboard nothings that generate zero sympathy. They simply never manage to generate the illusion of being real people -- probably because none of them have any credible backstory, their inter-relationships are painfully phoney, and, perhaps with the exception of the little girl, nothing really happens to generate any character development.

Plot twists? If only there was a plot to twist. As it is, the 'writer' just reheated tropes from the earlier JP films (mainly the first) and strung them together to serve as a 'story'. Virtually nothing generates any surprise or suspense or thrills.

As for humor, the high comedic moment is when Xavier goes off to pee in the jungle (replete with lengthy and graphic sound effects), freezes in mid-pee at the sound of a dinosaur battle behind him, and then finishes peeing when the battle is concluded. No doubt the writer remembered the classic outhouse scene in Jurassic Park I and thought that urination would be a hilarious adaptation. Let's be thankful, therefore, that no further efforts at comedic relief were attempted in this film.

Maybe worst of all, Scarlett Johansson fails utterly to lend credibility to the central 'hero' character Zora. Her boring lines and nasal vocal delivery are utterly at odds with the perform-under-pressure/save-the-day character that is required, so that when she finally springs into action with some angular kung-fu like poses, it only looks ridiculously contrived.

raw_payload.author: burgerpnk
raw_payload.content:
I'll qualify my review with a few things: 1) I went into this with very low expectations, and even with my positive review, I recommend you do the same. 2) I quite like The Lost World and Jurassic Park 3, even with their flaws. Nostalgia plays a heavy role though as I saw those when I was very young. 3) I thoroughly disliked all of the new Jurassic World movies and like most people, essentially swore off any movie-grab sequel. Yet here I am on day three of opening...

Without any spoilers past what's shown in trailers, there are still a lot of flaws in this movie that I'm sure every critic review will go into. The first 1/3 drags on and feels pretty forced. For some reason I'll never figure out, all Jurassic World movies have fallen into this ridiculous trap of screaming backstories at you and "telling not showing", and Rebirth is no exception. They could have worked in the backstories as the movie progressed like the Jurassic Park trilogy and essentially every "good" movie since the beginning of time does. Nope, back to "here's my entire story, person I already know deeply. I need you to remember these potentially tragic things you already know about me but somehow forgot". There are so many other ways to work those items in and yet again, we get a slow, unnatural feeling start. There are also some gags that are a bit much, guns being aimed to fire while empty, and we're still 100% in CGI world vs. The practical-CGI blend that made the original so incredible.

But that's where things shifted for me personally. As the movie progressed, it did what none of the Jurassic World movies before it could; it was actually entertaining. The dinosaur sequences in general were really impressive. CGI has come a long way since even the first Jurassic World, so everything looks drastically better. Dialogue and characters overall are much more natural than the last several, albeit still forced at times. Even though ScarJo and Mahershala Ali aren't exactly in their most challenging or deep roles, it's just such a joy watching them, as well as Manuel Garcia-Rolfo and Jonathan Bailey. Compared to three movies watching Chris Pratt and Dallas Howard put in an audition for Days of Our Lives, it's a dramatic improvement.

The biggest success, and why I think this is so worth seeing in theaters, is they nailed the dinosaur-heavy, stressful scenes. That hasn't once been accomplished in any Jurassic World movie, but nearly every one of these scenes were intense. They have some great homages without any overkill (including a book reference or two), mix in new things, and again have drastically improved CGI. How anyone can watch the raptors in the other Jurassic Worlds and not either laugh or get up and leave is beyond me. That's DRASTICALLY improved here, and let's be honest; that's the reason we watch any of these movies past the original.

Overall, just a massive improvement vs. The last three and fun movie that stands on its own. Personal rankings are:

1) Jurassic Park 2) Lost World 3) Jurassic Park 3 4) Rebirth 5) Congo (closer to Jurassic Park than the Jurassic World trilogy and obviously Crichton so I'm canonizing it) 6) Jurassic World 7) Dominion 8) Fallen Kingdom.

raw_payload.author: davewmullins2003

As a 36 year old man, that was at a prime age for the release of all of the original JP's, I live for the nostalgia.

As the films went on, we were hit with decline after decline.

What I liked: * The build-up to this films release - the wonder of whether they could possibly bring back that nostalgia and hit me with some good old fashioned Jurassic Park magic.

What I disliked: *The poor dialogue *The clichés (endless) *The questionable acting *The characters (world renowned special op that cannot hit a target and struggles with premature firing of a rifle) *The poor excuse for CGI - the original made you believe, and still does to this day! There's no Jurassic magic. It's gone, dead, apparently never to be seen again.

To the creators, please, from the bottom of my heart, stop ruining my adulthood.

raw_payload.author: harry_tk_yung
raw_payload.content:
Let me started with some numbers. When a mega pharmaceutical outfit executive Martin Krebs (Rupert Friend) offers top-notch mercenary Zora Bennett (Scarlett Johansson) "deep pocket" reward for an assignment, she says she wants a number. "Ten, with six zeroes" comes the answer. "Do the zeroes include the one in the ten?" a logical follow-up question. "No", a straight answer that gets her attention. But not too long into the movie, the audience witness that what ten million dollars gets is someone who manages to miss a target, a dinosaur considerably bigger than the boats they are on. From that point on, I lowered my expectation bar. Still, it is reasonably entertaining, but offers nothing new, just checking off the boxes from all the previous tours to the Dinosaur Universe.

The "trillion-dollar" project involves going to an equatorial island to get samples of dinosaur blood. This is 32 years after Jurassic Park, when dinosaurs have become a nuisance rather than a curiosity interests. Fortunately for humans, the narrow equatorial belt provides the only environment where they can survive and so they are left alone, let be. But scientists have found that DNAs from certain such species can be developed into a miracle drug for heart disease. One such scientists Henry Loomis (Jonathan Bailey) naturally becomes the third member the team, to get blood samples from dinosaurs. A fourth is Zora's former fellow mercenary Duncan Kincaid (Mahershala Ali) who owns a boat and knows the area. There are of course various crew members but they are just moving stage props and most of them become dinosaur food eventually.

And then, there is subplot that just about every critic calls a waste of time (or maybe fillers of time, running time, that is, of the movie). It is a family that gets rescued by our exploration party after their boat is capsized by a dinosaur. This is a "family" of 4, the quotation mark denoting that it is parents and daughter, plus mother's tag-along, lazy "boyfriend". I'll just quote these lines to complete my description of the family. Mother to father, about her boyfriend "there is another side of him you haven't seen". Without batting an eye, the little girl quips "The naked side!" There, you have it. Will they become dinosaur food too? I'll let you find out for yourself.

The actors, all veterans and one with an Oscar, are all wasted because of the thin script. But I guess you could say that it would be even worse without them, and you are right. The technology side though is good, in some cases even better than the six predecessors. But that is to be expected as the first one, after all, was 32 years ago. While on that, there is one throwback scene, not to the old Jurassic Park era, but to the more recent reboot of Jurassic World. It is someone lighting up a flare to attract a dinosaur to follow. Here it is a macho man (Mahershala Ali) risking his life to save his friends. The retro scene from the recent reboot is a gorgeous woman (Bryce Dallas Howard), trying to bring a "friendly" dinosaur to battle a deadly opponent, again with the same purpose, to save her friends. Both offer a same magical cinematic moment when the flare in the hand of the actor lights up.

raw_payload.author: DeadShot28
raw_payload.content:
Jurassic World Rebirth attempts to breathe new life into the franchise with a sleek cast and a high-stakes premise, but the results are a mixed for me.

Scarlett Johansson leads the charge as Zora, tasked with assembling a team to infiltrate a dinosaur-infested island. Their mission? Extract genetic samples from key species to engineer a cure that could change the world. It's a classic Jurassic setup science meets survival in a prehistoric nightmare but with a more focused, character driven approach.

One of the film's standouts is Mahershala Ali as Duncan. Every moment he's on screen crackles with presence and quiet intensity. Frankly, it felt like he could have carried the entire film solo. His performance adds weight to a story that sometimes struggles to balance its tones.

Unfortunately, not all characters are created equal. Four supporting characters, introduced as a stranded family unit, feel oddly disconnected from the main storyline. Their subplot often filled with poorly written dialogue and awkward humor feels like it belongs in a different movie entirely. While they do intersect with the main group eventually, their scenes dilute the tension and momentum built by Zora's team.

David Iacono's character, Xavier, is particularly uneven. He has several flat comedic lines that feel forced, yet he redeems himself with a heartfelt bonding scene that briefly injects some genuine emotion into the film.

The action is solid, and the island setting is as thrilling and dangerous as ever, but tonal inconsistencies and unnecessary characters bog it down. It's fun, sure but forgettable. It's what I'd call a "popcorn movie" entertaining enough at the moment, but unlikely to leave a lasting impression. If this ends up being the last entry in the Jurassic saga, I won't feel like we missed out on anything groundbreaking.

raw_payload.author: HashinthaL
raw_payload.content:
This honestly feels like they tried to integrate AI just for the sake of it, but it completely misses the mark of what a 2025 movie should look like. Visually and emotionally, it doesn't hold up. The storytelling feels artificial and the sense of wonder that once defined this franchise is gone.

Jurassic World already took a big hit after Fallen Kingdom, but this new attempt feels like the final nail in the coffin. It's like watching the slow and painful death of a legendary movie series.

To be fair, movies like Jurassic Park, Anaconda and King Kong managed to create suspense, emotion and a real connection with the creatures.

This one, by contrast, feels hollow and disconnected.

In Jurassic World Fallen Kingdom, the dinosaurs felt real, alive, and even majestic. There was depth and visual authenticity.

Then came Jurassic World Dominion, which took it a step further by presenting the dinosaurs as intelligent beings a bold, if controversial, move.

But now, in 2025, this so-called "Rebirth" just feels like AI Instead of reviving the magic, it's making us question why the franchise was resurrected at all. It lacks vision, emotional impact, and genuine creativity.

I really hope the next movie isn't like this. The Jurassic universe deserves better - better storytelling, better visuals, and better respect for what made the original movies timeless. Until then, maybe it's time to let the dinosaurs rest in peace.

Grant and Saddler first saw dinosaurs. In my opinion, this is a solid addition to the franchise.

raw_payload.author: Marlon2008
raw_payload.content:
I'll be honest - 6 stars might already be a bit generous, but let's start with the positives. The few scenes where dinosaurs did appear were absolutely stunning. Those moments gave me that old-school Jurassic feeling: tense, chilling, and downright terrifying in the best way. Just like the original films, they reminded you that these creatures aren't just animals - they're forces of nature. That part was brilliant.

But the problem is: these scenes were rare. Most of the film felt more like a drama than a dinosaur adventure. There was a lot of emotional dialogue, personal struggles, and scenic jungle shots. Honestly, I saw more water and trees than actual dinosaurs. At some point, I was wondering if this was Jurassic World or just Emotional Wilderness: The Rebirth.

It's impressive how far the franchise has come - I really respect the effort behind keeping it alive for so long. But with this film, I felt like the spirit of Jurassic was barely there. The stakes didn't feel high, and the story didn't give me any real excitement or thrill like the earlier movies did. I don't even know where they could take the series from here without recycling the same emotional arcs or just rehashing old ideas.

If you're a hardcore fan, it might still be worth watching for the nostalgia and those short, intense dinosaur scenes. But if you came for non-stop prehistoric action... you'll probably be disappointed.

raw_payload.author: leecad82

Rich folk want more money and dinosaurs are the answer. Random team put together to get what the rich folk need.

Horror results ... big bad dinosaurs chase the humans. Throw in a random family who are lost at sea and random team members start getting a conscience.

If you want jumpy, horror style animal chase movie with big CGI dinosaurs then fill your boots. Oh and the family are some kind of extreme survivors. A lot of the action is based at night in a jungle full of mutated dinosaurs but of course that's no issue for these folk.

raw_payload.author: IanJamesMitchell
raw_payload.content:
This was fine, mostly, but in comparison to Jurassic World and Fallen Kingdom, this was a disappointment, and, quite frankly, a million miles away from the original classic.

Better than Dominion, which isn't saying much, the writing of this felt rather rushed - which I'm pretty sure was its main failing. Despite a great cast and wonderful director, Gareth Edwards did all he could to roll the screenplay in glitter, but it still came out as a bit of a turd.

Scarlett Johansson and co really try, but the dialogue is so wooden and the character development almost paper-thin. Edwards earns his money by creating some memorable action sequences and it's great to see some of the new mutant-beasties on the big screen, but the whole thing is very linear without any reference to the wider universe. Despite its efforts, it's also quite humourless; the beats just don't quite work and it's lacking the comedic gravitas of previous casts gone by.

If you've had a hard day and have already consumed half a bottle of wine, this is perfectly adequate entertainment, however, if you were hoping for something that lived up to the trailers, maybe revisit one of the previous six entries of this tired franchise instead.
